skill.name: wcag-audit-patterns
skill.description: Conduct WCAG 2.2 accessibility audits with automated checks, manual review steps, and remediation guidance for web interfaces.
skill.summary: Guide accessibility audits from automated scans through manual WCAG verification and remediation planning.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static findings were evaluated against the skill content. Most high and critical alerts are false positives caused by Markdown fences, WCAG level labels, keyboard examples, and documentation links; the confirmed residual risk is legitimate use of external accessibility scanning commands against web URLs.

medium_findings.title: External Accessibility Scans Require Authorized Targets
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run automated scans and gives CLI examples using axe, pa11y, and Lighthouse against web URLs. This is legitimate audit behavior, but it can contact external sites and should only be used on authorized targets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly instruct running accessibility scanners and show commands that access URLs. The context is accessibility testing, not exfiltration or unauthorized reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: The external command detections largely point to Markdown code fences and documentation examples. No Ruby backtick execution or hidden runnable script behavior was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is plain Markdown documentation or JavaScript sample code. There is no evidence that the skill itself executes these fenced blocks automatically.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Credential Alerts Are Accessibility Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts match WCAG level labels such as A, AA, and AAA. The certificate or key alerts match accessibility keyboard examples, including keydown handling, not private keys or certificates.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Semantic context confirms the flagged strings are WCAG conformance levels and keyboard interaction examples. No cryptographic code, certificate material, or secret value appears in the reviewed content.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Are Benign References
low_findings.description: The network findings include example scan targets and public accessibility reference links. No credential transmission, tracking endpoint, or suspicious domain was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are example.com and known accessibility resources. They are visible documentation references, although the scanner command examples still justify a medium usage warning.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed After Context Review
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined code fences, URLs, and key-related words, but context shows accessibility documentation. No prompt injection, malicious override text, credential access, or data exfiltration pattern was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files describe a WCAG audit workflow and linked playbook. I found no evidence of hidden instructions, malicious network behavior, or attempts to override the marketplace audit process.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Run CLI Commands Against Web URLs
dangerous_patterns.description: The playbook includes command examples that run accessibility tools against URLs. These commands are transparent and useful, but users should limit scans to systems they own or are authorized to test.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The bash block clearly shows external scanning commands. The commands are standard accessibility tools and do not include credential handling or suspicious destinations.

content.value_statement: Accessibility audits need both automated scans and manual verification. This skill guides WCAG 2.2 checks, issue triage, and remediation planning.
actual_capabilities: Maps accessibility issues to WCAG 2.2 principles and success criteria.
Combines automated tool results with manual keyboard and screen reader checks.
Provides remediation patterns for labels, contrast, focus, landmarks, and custom widgets.
Helps structure audit evidence with severity, test steps, and residual risk.
Supports re-testing workflows after accessibility fixes are implemented.
limitations: Does not provide legal advice or formal accessibility certification.
Cannot prove full conformance from automated scan results alone.
Requires access to the interface, source, or test environment for useful remediation.
External scans should only target systems the user is authorized to test.

use_cases.title: Audit a Product Release
use_cases.description: Run a WCAG 2.2 review before launch and document issues by severity and success criterion.

use_cases.title: Fix Component Accessibility
use_cases.description: Use remediation patterns to correct labels, focus behavior, contrast, and custom widget semantics.

use_cases.title: Prepare Compliance Evidence
use_cases.description: Organize findings, test steps, re-test notes, and residual risk for Section 508 or VPAT preparation.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this page for WCAG 2.2 AA issues. Separate automated findings from manual checks and explain each issue in plain language.

prompt_templates.title: Review Keyboard Access
prompt_templates.prompt: Check this workflow for keyboard accessibility. Report focus order, visible focus, traps, skipped controls, and recommended fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Triage Scan Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these accessibility scan results. Group duplicates, map each issue to WCAG criteria, assign severity, and list verification steps.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Remediation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a remediation plan for these WCAG findings. Include affected components, implementation guidance, acceptance checks, owner roles, and re-test evidence.

output_examples.input: A checkout form has placeholder-only fields and no visible labels.
output_examples.output: The audit would flag missing labels, map the issue to WCAG 1.3.1 and 3.3.2, and recommend visible label text.

output_examples.input: A modal opens, but focus stays behind the dialog.
output_examples.output: The report would identify a keyboard blocker, explain the expected focus behavior, and list checks for opening and closing the modal.

output_examples.input: A Lighthouse report shows several contrast failures.
output_examples.output: The skill would group related contrast issues, distinguish text from component contrast, and define re-test steps after color updates.

best_practices: Test with automated tools first, then verify important flows manually.
Record the page, component, WCAG criterion, severity, and reproduction steps for each issue.
Re-test every fix with the same method used to confirm the original failure.
anti_patterns: Do not claim full compliance from an automated scan alone.
Do not remove focus outlines without providing an equally visible replacement.
Do not scan third-party systems unless you have explicit authorization.

faq.question: Does this skill certify WCAG compliance?
faq.answer: No. It supports audit work and remediation planning, but formal certification needs qualified human review.

faq.question: Which WCAG version does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on WCAG 2.2, with practical checks for common Level A and AA requirements.

faq.question: Can it replace manual accessibility testing?
faq.answer: No. Automated tools catch many issues, but keyboard flow, screen reader behavior, and usability need manual review.

faq.question: What tools does it mention?
faq.answer: It references common tools such as axe, Lighthouse, WAVE, and pa11y as part of a broader audit process.

faq.question: Is it safe to use on production sites?
faq.answer: Use it only on sites you own or are authorized to test. Prefer staging environments when possible.

faq.question: What output should users expect?
faq.answer: Users should expect prioritized findings, WCAG mapping, remediation guidance, and re-test steps.
